{"action":"closed","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/holoviz/holoviews/issues/6460","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/holoviz/holoviews","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/holoviz/holoviews/issues/6460/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/holoviz/holoviews/issues/6460/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/holoviz/holoviews/issues/6460/events","html_url":"https://github.com/holoviz/holoviews/issues/6460","id":2702523229,"node_id":"I_kwDOASoy8M6hFTtd","number":6460,"title":"hv.QuadMesh - strange behavior with the `vdim` parameter","user":{"login":"MatusGasparik","id":15058994,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1MDU4OTk0","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/15058994?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/MatusGasparik","html_url":"https://github.com/MatusGasparik","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MatusGasparik/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MatusGasparik/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MatusGasparik/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MatusGasparik/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MatusGasparik/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MatusGasparik/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MatusGasparik/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MatusGasparik/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MatusGasparik/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":97850376,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw5Nzg1MDM3Ng==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/holoviz/holoviews/labels/type:%20bug","name":"type: bug","color":"fc2929","default":false,"description":"Something isn't correct or isn't working"}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-11-28T15:33:25Z","updated_at":"2024-12-02T10:28:06Z","closed_at":"2024-12-02T10:28:05Z","author_association":"NONE","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":" #### ALL software version info (this library, plus any other relevant software, e.g. bokeh, python, notebook, OS, browser, etc should be added within the dropdown below.)
Software Version Info ```plaintext holoviews: 1.19.1 bokeh: 3.5.2 python: 3.11.10 jupyterlab: 4.2.5 OS: macOS 12.7.5 Browser: Chrome ```
#### Description of expected behavior and the observed behavior Example code for `QuadMesh` from the docs - slightly modified by using explicit `kdims` and `vdims`, working as expected: ```python import numpy as np import holoviews as hv hv.extension('bokeh') n = 8 # Number of bins in each direction xs = np.logspace(1, 3, n) ys = np.linspace(1, 10, n) zs = np.arange((n-1)**2).reshape(n-1, n-1) hv.QuadMesh((xs, ys, zs), ['x', 'y'], ['foo']) ``` Result: #### Complete, minimal, self-contained example code that reproduces the issue Same code but having a name for the `vdim` that isn't a valid variable name in python (such as `' foo (unit)' ` produces a blank plot (no error) ```python n = 8 # Number of bins in each direction xs = np.logspace(1, 3, n) ys = np.linspace(1, 10, n) zs = np.arange((n-1)**2).reshape(n-1, n-1) hv.QuadMesh((xs, ys, zs), ['x', 'y'], ['foo (unit)']) ``` Result: I tried this with other element types (...actually only with `hv.Curve`) and a `vdim` name that is not a valid python name still works as expected: ```python xs = range(-10,11) ys = [100-x**2 for x in xs] hv.Curve((xs, ys), 'foo', 'bar (unit)') ``` Result: So this likely an issue with `QuadMesh` specifically. Any ideas? #### One more strangeness Strangely enough, the above example that resulted in a blank `QuadMesh` plot, suddenly works when the X and Y dimensions are 2D instead of 1D: ```python n = 8 # Number of bins in each direction xs = np.logspace(1, 3, n) ys = np.linspace(1, 10, n) zs = np.arange((n-1)**2).reshape(n-1, n-1) XX, YY = np.meshgrid(xs, ys) hv.QuadMesh((XX, YY, zs), ['x', 'y'], 'foo (unit)') ``` Result: Now, I am genuinely puzzled by what's going on... versions:
- holoviews = 1.16.1

As of Bokeh 3.0, [LaTeX is now supported](https://docs.bokeh.org/en/3.1.1/docs/user_guide/styling/mathtext.html), but I don't think it's supported by holoviews yet. Using `r"Upwelling Longwave Flux W$$m^{-2}$$"` doesn't work for the colorbar label:

![test_olr](https://github.com/holoviz/holoviews/assets/66640080/f7ca8079-2af0-41c3-b4aa-de3bb6ecc3a0)

And using that same label but for the title throws a KeyError:

<details>
<summary>Bokeh Code</summary>

``` python
import numpy as np
from bokeh.io import output_notebook
from bokeh.plotting import figure, show

output_notebook()
N = 4000
x = np.random.random(size=N) * 100
y = np.random.random(size=N) * 100
radii = np.random.random(size=N) * 1.5
colors = np.array([(r, g, 150) for r, g in zip(50 + 2 * x, 30 + 2 * y)], dtype="uint8")

p = figure(width=300, height=300)
p.circle(x, y, radius=radii, fill_color=colors, fill_alpha=0.6, line_color=None)

p.xaxis.axis_label = r"$$ \text{ This works } \times 10^6$$"

show(p)
```

</details>

Example:
1. The code waits for 2 seconds before showing the loading icon.
2. If the process completes or the event resolves within those 2 seconds, the icon is never shown.
3. If the process doesn't complete, the icon appears after the delay, signaling that something is still loading.


This feature could be particularly useful for applications with frequent, rapid user interactions that trigger parameter updates (e.g., sliders or live data streams). It would improve the UX by removing flicker.

Use case would be linking widgets with `self.state.param.busy`, but at the current moment it could cause flickering:
https://github.com/holoviz/lumen/pull/783#discussion_r1868152821

I think it would be implemented here https://github.com/holoviz/param/blob/main/param/parameterized.py#L2297-L2357","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/holoviz/param/issues/984/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/holoviz/param/issues/984/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/holoviz/param/issues/comments/2517457251","html_url":"https://github.com/holoviz/param/issues/984#issuecomment-2517457251","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/holoviz/param/issues/984","id":2517457251,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAD_2Sc6WDVlj","user":{"login":"hoxbro","id":19758978,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE5NzU4OTc4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/19758978?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro","html_url":"https://github.com/hoxbro","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-04T13:49:06Z","updated_at":"2024-12-04T13:49:06Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"I don't think this functionality should live in Param. #### ALL software version info
(this library, plus any other relevant software, e.g. bokeh, python, notebook, OS, browser, etc should be added within the dropdown below.)

<details>
    <summary>Software Version Info</summary>

```plaintext
holoviews:  1.19.1 
bokeh: 3.5.2
python: 3.11.10
jupyterlab: 4.2.5

OS: macOS 12.7.5 
Browser: Chrome
```
</details>

#### Description of expected behavior and the observed behavior

I am creating a rasterized version of hv.QuadMesh element to visualize a Power Spectrogram for a long audio file (>30 mins). I set the `x_sampling` parameter to significantly higher value that the `y_sampling` as I intent to view the spectrogram at different zoom levels of the x-axis. And all seems to work as expected.

```python
spec_hv = hv.QuadMesh(
    (times, freqs_log2, S_dB),
    kdims=[time_dim, log_freq_dim],
    vdims=[power_dim], label='Spec', group='Image'
)

spec_rast = rasterize(spec_hv, x_sampling=0.001, y_sampling=0.05).opts(
    width=800, height=300,
    cmap='Viridis', colorbar=True,
    title='Log Power Spectrogram',
    clabel='Amplitude (dB)',
).relabel(group='Image', label='Spec')

spec_rast
```

Overview:

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/618ddc03-7ef9-496b-a483-6b65108208be)

Zoomed in (first 100 seconds):

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/0033c29d-2861-4d79-8a88-ef71c5ca197f)


But, as soon as I try to use the rasterized element with other elements in hv.Layout, the resolution is lost.

```python
wheel_zoom = WheelZoomTool(dimensions='width')
box_zoom = BoxZoomTool(dimensions='width')
pan = PanTool(dimensions='width')

tools = dict(
    default_tools=[pan, wheel_zoom, box_zoom, 'reset'],
    #active_tools=[pan, wheel_zoom]
)

# RangeToolLink functionality
# but with mutliple targets
RTM = RangeToolManager()


layout = (
    waveform.opts(hooks=[RTM.add_range_tool]) + \
    rms_hv.opts(hooks=[RTM.link_x_range_to_primary]) + \
    spec_rast.opts(hooks=[RTM.link_x_range_to_primary])
).cols(1).opts(shared_axes=False, merge_tools=True)

layout.opts(
    opts.Curve('Line.Waveform', color='blue', width=WIDTH, height=120, line_width=0.6, xlabel='', default_tools=[]),
    opts.Curve('Line.RMS', color='black',  xlabel='', line_width=1.2, **tools),
    opts.Image('Image.Spec', **tools),
)
```

Overview:

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/1efb9679-cb3d-4329-8d44-5e9731b7a1ec)

Zoomed in (first 100 seconds):

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/82f7e3df-1d8e-42b0-b008-f59f66eac07a)

I'm not sure if I'm doing something wrong or of this is a bug.

import holoviews as hv
from holoviews.plotting.links import RangeToolLink
import numpy as np

hv.extension('bokeh')

x = np.linspace(0, 10, 100)
y = np.sin(x)

plot0 = hv.Curve((x, y)).opts(title="Source")
plot1 = hv.Curve((x, y)).opts(title="Target 1")
plot2 = hv.Curve((x, y)).opts(title="Target 2")

RangeToolLink(plot0, plot1, axes=['x','y'])

plot0 + plot1 + plot2

```

``` python
import numpy as np
from bokeh.models import BoxZoomTool, PanTool, WheelZoomTool

import holoviews as hv
from holoviews import opts
from holoviews.operation.datashader import rasterize

hv.extension("bokeh")

times = np.linspace(0, 1800, 5000)
freqs = np.linspace(20, 20000, 256)
freqs_log2 = np.log2(freqs)

T, F = np.meshgrid(times, freqs_log2)
S_dB = (np.sin(T / 5) + np.random.normal(0, 0.2, T.shape)) * np.cos(F / 10) * 20

spec_hv = hv.QuadMesh(
    (times, freqs_log2, S_dB),
    kdims=["Time (s)", "Log Frequency (log₂ Hz)"],
    vdims=["Amplitude (dB)"],
    label="Spec",
    group="Image",
)

spec_rast = (
    rasterize(spec_hv, x_sampling=0.001, y_sampling=0.05)
    .opts(
        width=800,
        height=300,
        cmap="Viridis",
        colorbar=True,
        title="Log Power Spectrogram",
        clabel="Amplitude (dB)",
    )
    .relabel(group="Image", label="Spec")
)


waveform_times = np.linspace(0, 1800, 5000)
waveform_amplitude = np.sin(waveform_times / 50)

waveform = hv.Curve(
    (waveform_times, waveform_amplitude), "Time (s)", "Amplitude", label="Waveform"
)

rms_values = np.abs(waveform_amplitude)

rms_hv = hv.Curve((waveform_times, rms_values), "Time (s)", "RMS", label="RMS")

wheel_zoom = WheelZoomTool(dimensions="width")
box_zoom = BoxZoomTool(dimensions="width")
pan = PanTool(dimensions="width")

tools = dict(
    default_tools=[pan, wheel_zoom, box_zoom, "reset"],
)

WIDTH = 800

layout = (waveform.opts(**tools) + rms_hv + spec_rast.opts(**tools)).cols(1)
RangeToolLink(waveform, spec_rast, axes=["x"])

layout.opts(
    opts.Curve(
        "Curve.Waveform",
        color="blue",
        width=WIDTH,
        height=120,
    ),
    opts.Curve(
        "Curve.RMS",
        color="black",
        width=WIDTH,
        height=120,
    ),
    opts.Image(
        "Spec",
        width=WIDTH,
        **tools,
    ),
)
layout
```

np.random.seed(1)

data = np.random.randn(1000).cumsum()
x1 = np.arange(data.size)
x2 = x1 * 0.5

source = hv.Curve((x1, data)).opts(
    width=800, height=125, axiswise=True, default_tools=[]
)

``` python
import numpy as np
from bokeh.models import BoxZoomTool, PanTool, WheelZoomTool

import holoviews as hv
from holoviews import opts
from holoviews.operation.datashader import rasterize

hv.extension("bokeh")

times = np.linspace(0, 1800, 5000)
freqs = np.linspace(20, 20000, 256)
freqs_log2 = np.log2(freqs)

T, F = np.meshgrid(times, freqs_log2)
S_dB = (np.sin(T / 5) + np.random.normal(0, 0.2, T.shape)) * np.cos(F / 10) * 20

spec_hv = hv.QuadMesh(
    (times, freqs_log2, S_dB),
    kdims=["Time (s)", "Log Frequency (log₂ Hz)"],
    vdims=["Amplitude (dB)"],
    label="Spec",
    group="Image",
)

spec_rast = (
    rasterize(spec_hv, x_sampling=0.001, y_sampling=0.05)
    .opts(
        width=800,
        height=300,
        cmap="Viridis",
        colorbar=True,
        title="Log Power Spectrogram",
        clabel="Amplitude (dB)",
    )
    .relabel(group="Image", label="Spec")
)


waveform_times = np.linspace(0, 1800, 5000)
waveform_amplitude = np.sin(waveform_times / 50)

waveform = hv.Curve(
    (waveform_times, waveform_amplitude), "Time (s)", "Amplitude", label="Waveform"
)

rms_values = np.abs(waveform_amplitude)

rms_hv = hv.Curve((waveform_times, rms_values), "Time (s)", "RMS", label="RMS")

wheel_zoom = WheelZoomTool(dimensions="width")
box_zoom = BoxZoomTool(dimensions="width")
pan = PanTool(dimensions="width")

tools = dict(
    default_tools=[pan, wheel_zoom, box_zoom, "reset"],
)

WIDTH = 800

layout = (waveform.opts(**tools) + rms_hv + spec_rast.opts(**tools)).cols(1)
RangeToolLink(waveform, spec_rast, axes=["x"])

layout.opts(
    opts.Curve(
        "Curve.Waveform",
        color="blue",
        width=WIDTH,
        height=120,
    ),
    opts.Curve(
        "Curve.RMS",
        color="black",
        width=WIDTH,
        height=120,
    ),
    opts.Image(
        "Spec",
        width=WIDTH,
        **tools,
    ),
)
layout
```

#### Description of expected behavior and the observed behavior

I am creating a rasterized version of hv.QuadMesh element to visualize a Power Spectrogram for a long audio file (>30 mins). I set the `x_sampling` parameter to significantly higher value that the `y_sampling` as I intent to view the spectrogram at different zoom levels of the x-axis. And all seems to work as expected.

```python
spec_hv = hv.QuadMesh(
    (times, freqs_log2, S_dB),
    kdims=[time_dim, log_freq_dim],
    vdims=[power_dim], label='Spec', group='Image'
)

spec_rast = rasterize(spec_hv, x_sampling=0.001, y_sampling=0.05).opts(
    width=800, height=300,
    cmap='Viridis', colorbar=True,
    title='Log Power Spectrogram',
    clabel='Amplitude (dB)',
).relabel(group='Image', label='Spec')

spec_rast
```

Overview:

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/618ddc03-7ef9-496b-a483-6b65108208be)

Zoomed in (first 100 seconds):

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/0033c29d-2861-4d79-8a88-ef71c5ca197f)


But, as soon as I try to use the rasterized element with other elements in hv.Layout, the resolution is lost.

```python
wheel_zoom = WheelZoomTool(dimensions='width')
box_zoom = BoxZoomTool(dimensions='width')
pan = PanTool(dimensions='width')

tools = dict(
    default_tools=[pan, wheel_zoom, box_zoom, 'reset'],
    #active_tools=[pan, wheel_zoom]
)

# RangeToolLink functionality
# but with mutliple targets
RTM = RangeToolManager()


layout = (
    waveform.opts(hooks=[RTM.add_range_tool]) + \
    rms_hv.opts(hooks=[RTM.link_x_range_to_primary]) + \
    spec_rast.opts(hooks=[RTM.link_x_range_to_primary])
).cols(1).opts(shared_axes=False, merge_tools=True)

layout.opts(
    opts.Curve('Line.Waveform', color='blue', width=WIDTH, height=120, line_width=0.6, xlabel='', default_tools=[]),
    opts.Curve('Line.RMS', color='black',  xlabel='', line_width=1.2, **tools),
    opts.Image('Image.Spec', **tools),
)
```

Overview:

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/1efb9679-cb3d-4329-8d44-5e9731b7a1ec)

Zoomed in (first 100 seconds):

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/82f7e3df-1d8e-42b0-b008-f59f66eac07a)

I'm not sure if I'm doing something wrong or of this is a bug.

    <summary>Software Version Info</summary>
```plaintext
holoviews 1.20.0
Bokeh 3.5.2
```
</details>

QuadMesh elements display fine gray or white lines between individual cells. From the docs:

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/19e982c9-125b-4147-99aa-eb0c2ef89c5a)

While with matplotlib backend, this is the default:
![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/1d90c7cb-d9d8-4145-a2d4-1cd962e811f5)

When you have small cells with respect to plot size, these separating lines can really mess with the apparent colours (I think this may be what is seen [here](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/72448687/python-using-hvplot-quadmesh-for-xarray-data-shows-blended-colors-in-a-plot)). Even in the general case, I'd like to be able to have my `hv.QuadMesh` yield the same visual output as my `hv.Image`, i.e. a continuous area instead of patches of colour detached from each other.

However, I have not found a way to do that. Clearly grid lines are not to blame. Then I tried setting `line_alpha` and `line_width` to 0, which from [this issue](https://github.com/holoviz/holoviews/issues/4016) I thought would work, to no avail. Maybe this can be done with a hook.

Interestingly I have the result I want by default using pure Bokeh (with the `figure.quad` glyph which is used by Holoviews) whether you create a multi-cell quad or aggregating several quads.

MRE:

```python
import numpy as np
import holoviews as hv

hv.extension('bokeh')

n = 8  # Number of bins in each direction
xs = np.logspace(1, 3, n)
ys = np.linspace(1, 10, n)
zs = np.arange((n-1)**2).reshape(n-1, n-1)
hv.QuadMesh((xs, ys, zs)).opts(line_width=0, line_alpha=0)
```

- [x] I may be interested in making a pull request to address this

    <summary>Software Version Info</summary>
```plaintext
holoviews 1.20.0
Bokeh 3.5.2
```
</details>

QuadMesh elements display fine gray or white lines between individual cells. From the docs:

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/19e982c9-125b-4147-99aa-eb0c2ef89c5a)

While with matplotlib backend, this is the default:
![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/1d90c7cb-d9d8-4145-a2d4-1cd962e811f5)

When you have small cells with respect to plot size, these separating lines can really mess with the apparent colours (I think this may be what is seen [here](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/72448687/python-using-hvplot-quadmesh-for-xarray-data-shows-blended-colors-in-a-plot)). Even in the general case, I'd like to be able to have my `hv.QuadMesh` yield the same visual output as my `hv.Image`, i.e. a continuous area instead of patches of colour detached from each other.

However, I have not found a way to do that. Clearly grid lines are not to blame. Then I tried setting `line_alpha` and `line_width` to 0, which from [this issue](https://github.com/holoviz/holoviews/issues/4016) I thought would work, to no avail. Maybe this can be done with a hook.

Interestingly I have the result I want by default using pure Bokeh (with the `figure.quad` glyph which is used by Holoviews) whether you create a multi-cell quad or aggregating several quads.

MRE:

```python
import numpy as np
import holoviews as hv

hv.extension('bokeh')

n = 8  # Number of bins in each direction
xs = np.logspace(1, 3, n)
ys = np.linspace(1, 10, n)
zs = np.arange((n-1)**2).reshape(n-1, n-1)
hv.QuadMesh((xs, ys, zs)).opts(line_width=0, line_alpha=0)
```

- [x] I may be interested in making a pull request to address this

faster spatialpandas unique scalar values","user":{"login":"maximlt","id":35924738,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM1OTI0NzM4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/35924738?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt","html_url":"https://github.com/maximlt","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"While working on modernizing the NYC Buildings example (https://github.com/holoviz-topics/examples/pull/386), I noticed that the creation of a HoloViews object (not the rendering) was very slow, the code below took  ~35 seconds to run on my machine.

```python
import time
import spatialpandas as spd
import spatialpandas.io
import hvplot.pandas # noqa

sdf = spd.io.read_parquet('/Users/mliquet/dev/examples/nyc_buildings/data/nyc_buildings.parq')
cats = ['unknown'] + list(sdf['type'].value_counts().iloc[:10].index.values)
sdf['type'] = sdf['type'].replace({None: 'unknown'})
sdf = sdf[sdf['type'].isin(cats)]
sdf['type'] = sdf['type'].astype('category')
sdf = sdf.build_sindex()

start = time.perf_counter()
print("Create object")
p = sdf.hvplot.polygons(rasterize=True, groupby='type', aggregator='any', width=600, height=500)
print(f"Obj creation time: {time.perf_counter() - start}")
```

The new code differs quite a bit from the old one, that was pure HoloViews. Note that the NYC Buildings example uses a DaskSpatialPandas object, I saw the problem was also present for a standard SpatialPandas object.

```python
cats = ['unknown'] + list(sdf['type'].value_counts().iloc[:10].index.values)
polys = hv.Polygons(sdf, vdims='type')
hmap  = hv.HoloMap(OrderedDict([(cat, polys.select(type=cat)) for cat in cats]), 'Type', sort=False)
rasterize(hmap, aggregator='any').opts(width=600, height=500)
```

The main difference is that, internally, hvPlot creates a dynamic HoloViews `groupby` operation. This operation calls the `values` method of the interface, and it turns out that this is very slow for the spatialpandas one, since AFAIU, it has to deal with collecting the values that may be associated with every node of a geometry. This code is in `get_value_array` that loops through every row of the dataset, and every node of the geometry, and creates intermediate numpy arrays that are finally combined based on how the function is called.

This PR implements a couple of optimizations, the main one being in https://github.com/holoviz/holoviews/commit/3fc8935ebbb9772bf5c68296c0b02d9ad51c6f96 that skips the loop entirely if all the values in the targeted column are scalar and if the function should return unique values (`expanded=False`, `expanded=True` is a bit more difficult o deal with as the values must be separated with `np.nan`, and not the scope of this PR). With this change, the timing went down to 0.4s, about 90x values","user":{"login":"maximlt","id":35924738,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM1OTI0NzM4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/35924738?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt","html_url":"https://github.com/maximlt","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"While working on modernizing the NYC Buildings example (https://github.com/holoviz-topics/examples/pull/386), I noticed that the creation of a HoloViews object (not the rendering) was very slow, the code below took  ~35 seconds to run on my machine.

```python
import time
import spatialpandas as spd
import spatialpandas.io
import hvplot.pandas # noqa

sdf = spd.io.read_parquet('/Users/mliquet/dev/examples/nyc_buildings/data/nyc_buildings.parq')
cats = ['unknown'] + list(sdf['type'].value_counts().iloc[:10].index.values)
sdf['type'] = sdf['type'].replace({None: 'unknown'})
sdf = sdf[sdf['type'].isin(cats)]
sdf['type'] = sdf['type'].astype('category')
sdf = sdf.build_sindex()

start = time.perf_counter()
print("Create object")
p = sdf.hvplot.polygons(rasterize=True, groupby='type', aggregator='any', width=600, height=500)
print(f"Obj creation time: {time.perf_counter() - start}")
```

The new code differs quite a bit from the old one, that was pure HoloViews. Note that the NYC Buildings example uses a DaskSpatialPandas object, I saw the problem was also present for a standard SpatialPandas object.

```python
cats = ['unknown'] + list(sdf['type'].value_counts().iloc[:10].index.values)
polys = hv.Polygons(sdf, vdims='type')
hmap  = hv.HoloMap(OrderedDict([(cat, polys.select(type=cat)) for cat in cats]), 'Type', sort=False)
rasterize(hmap, aggregator='any').opts(width=600, height=500)
```

The main difference is that, internally, hvPlot creates a dynamic HoloViews `groupby` operation. This operation calls the `values` method of the interface, and it turns out that this is very slow for the spatialpandas one, since AFAIU, it has to deal with collecting the values that may be associated with every node of a geometry. This code is in `get_value_array` that loops through every row of the dataset, and every node of the geometry, and creates intermediate numpy arrays that are finally combined based on how the function is called.

This PR implements a couple of optimizations, the main one being in https://github.com/holoviz/holoviews/commit/3fc8935ebbb9772bf5c68296c0b02d9ad51c6f96 that skips the loop entirely if all the values in the targeted column are scalar and if the function should return unique values (`expanded=False`, `expanded=True` is a bit more difficult o deal with as the values must be separated with `np.nan`, and not the scope of this PR). With this change, the timing went down to 0.4s, about 90x values","user":{"login":"maximlt","id":35924738,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM1OTI0NzM4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/35924738?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt","html_url":"https://github.com/maximlt","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"While working on modernizing the NYC Buildings example (https://github.com/holoviz-topics/examples/pull/386), I noticed that the creation of a HoloViews object (not the rendering) was very slow, the code below took  ~35 seconds to run on my machine.

```python
import time
import spatialpandas as spd
import spatialpandas.io
import hvplot.pandas # noqa

sdf = spd.io.read_parquet('/Users/mliquet/dev/examples/nyc_buildings/data/nyc_buildings.parq')
cats = ['unknown'] + list(sdf['type'].value_counts().iloc[:10].index.values)
sdf['type'] = sdf['type'].replace({None: 'unknown'})
sdf = sdf[sdf['type'].isin(cats)]
sdf['type'] = sdf['type'].astype('category')
sdf = sdf.build_sindex()

start = time.perf_counter()
print("Create object")
p = sdf.hvplot.polygons(rasterize=True, groupby='type', aggregator='any', width=600, height=500)
print(f"Obj creation time: {time.perf_counter() - start}")
```

The new code differs quite a bit from the old one, that was pure HoloViews. Note that the NYC Buildings example uses a DaskSpatialPandas object, I saw the problem was also present for a standard SpatialPandas object.

```python
cats = ['unknown'] + list(sdf['type'].value_counts().iloc[:10].index.values)
polys = hv.Polygons(sdf, vdims='type')
hmap  = hv.HoloMap(OrderedDict([(cat, polys.select(type=cat)) for cat in cats]), 'Type', sort=False)
rasterize(hmap, aggregator='any').opts(width=600, height=500)
```

The main difference is that, internally, hvPlot creates a dynamic HoloViews `groupby` operation. This operation calls the `values` method of the interface, and it turns out that this is very slow for the spatialpandas one, since AFAIU, it has to deal with collecting the values that may be associated with every node of a geometry. This code is in `get_value_array` that loops through every row of the dataset, and every node of the geometry, and creates intermediate numpy arrays that are finally combined based on how the function is called.

This PR implements a couple of optimizations, the main one being in https://github.com/holoviz/holoviews/commit/3fc8935ebbb9772bf5c68296c0b02d9ad51c6f96 that skips the loop entirely if all the values in the targeted column are scalar and if the function should return unique values (`expanded=False`, `expanded=True` is a bit more difficult o deal with as the values must be separated with `np.nan`, and not the scope of this PR). With this change, the timing went down to 0.4s, about 90x Software Version Info ```plaintext Python: 3.12.7 (`uv` venv) Holoviews: 1.19.1 Bokeh: 3.6.0 Ubuntu 20.04.5 LTS Browser: Chrome ```
#### Description of expected behavior and the observed behavior I'm trying to plot multiple lines using `holoviews` with the `bokeh` back end showing additional hover information. I've settled on using `hv.Contours` for my use case***. To include the hover info, I added it to the `vdims` parameter. I expected this to result in showing my hover information, but if I specified for example `color="black"` as well, then the lines would still be black. (I would also expect the lines to be a single color by default for my use case, but I believe the primary use of `hv.Contours` is contours on an image, which probably should be colored by default, so that's fine.) In practice, although this sets my hover info as expected, it _also_ sets that vdim as the color of the lines, even if I specify a non-vdim line color (see example below). (Note: if I color the lines by an additional / different vdim, then the lines get colored as expected, which at least presents a natural hack solution on my end.) ***I did not include it in this minimal example, but I need to support _sometimes_ including line-specific viz kwargs on each line, for example line width, alpha, and / or color, which brought me to using `hv.Contours` instead of `hv.Curve` or `hv.Path`, both of which I tried before settling on `hv.Contours`. My lines are also composed of a potentially varying number of points (and always >2 points), which ruled out the use of `hv.Segments`. #### Complete, minimal, self-contained example code that reproduces the issue ```python import holoviews as hv import numpy as np from bokeh.models import HoverTool hv.extension("bokeh") # generate some random curves num_pts = 10 rng = np.random.default_rng(0) x = rng.random(size=num_pts * 3).reshape(-1, 3) y = rng.random(size=num_pts * 3).reshape(-1, 3) # generate some corresponding hover information hover_info_variable_name = "hover_info" hover_info = [f"Unique Hover Info: {i:.2f}" for i in rng.random(size=num_pts)] data_dicts = [ {"x": i, "y": j, hover_info_variable_name: k} for i, j, k in zip(x, y, hover_info) ] # custom tooltip for showing hover info tooltips = f"""
Custom Hover Info: @{{{hover_info_variable_name}}}
""" hover_tool = HoverTool( tooltips=tooltips, description="My Custom Hover Tool", ) # desired behavior: black lines with correct hover info # observed behavior: correct hover info, but lines colored by vdim curves_with_hover_info = hv.Contours( data_dicts, kdims=["x", "y"], vdims=[hover_info_variable_name], # include for hover info, NOT for color ).opts( show_legend=False, color="black", # this gets ignored tools=[hover_tool], ) # this case meets expected behavior: black lines but no hover info to correctly reference curves_without_hover_info = hv.Contours( data_dicts, kdims=["x", "y"], # skipping vdims here ).opts( show_legend=False, color="black", # this gets used now tools=[hover_tool], ) fig = curves_with_hover_info.opts( title="Hover info is correct\nbut colored by vdim" ) + curves_without_hover_info.opts(title="Color correct\nbut '???' hover info") fig # also ran as a script locally to confirm the issue wasn't from running in a jupyter notebook # hv.save(fig, "./contours_bug.html") ``` #### Stack traceback and/or browser JavaScript console output N/A #### Screenshots or screencasts of the bug in action ![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/565c726e-c35d-4dff-8863-6eb8063462f4) ![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/97da045e-25fc-4937-8566-f664281d549f) - [x] I may be interested in making a pull request to address this ","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/holoviz/holoviews/issues/6469/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/holoviz/holoviews/issues/6469/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/holoviz/holoviews/issues/comments/2528656854","html_url":"https://github.com/holoviz/holoviews/issues/6469#issuecomment-2528656854","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/holoviz/holoviews/issues/6469","id":2528656854,"node_id":"IC_kwDOASoy8M6WuD3W","user":{"login":"hoxbro","id":19758978,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE5NzU4OTc4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/19758978?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro","html_url":"https://github.com/hoxbro","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-09T16:42:59Z","updated_at":"2024-12-09T16:42:59Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"This is because we go down a bad route because of the legacy `color_index`.     <summary>Software Version Info</summary>
```plaintext
holoviews 1.20.0
Bokeh 3.5.2
```
</details>

QuadMesh elements display fine gray or white lines between individual cells. From the docs:

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/19e982c9-125b-4147-99aa-eb0c2ef89c5a)

While with matplotlib backend, this is the default:
![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/1d90c7cb-d9d8-4145-a2d4-1cd962e811f5)

When you have small cells with respect to plot size, these separating lines can really mess with the apparent colours (I think this may be what is seen [here](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/72448687/python-using-hvplot-quadmesh-for-xarray-data-shows-blended-colors-in-a-plot)). Even in the general case, I'd like to be able to have my `hv.QuadMesh` yield the same visual output as my `hv.Image`, i.e. a continuous area instead of patches of colour detached from each other.

However, I have not found a way to do that. Clearly grid lines are not to blame. Then I tried setting `line_alpha` and `line_width` to 0, which from [this issue](https://github.com/holoviz/holoviews/issues/4016) I thought would work, to no avail. Maybe this can be done with a hook.

Interestingly I have the result I want by default using pure Bokeh (with the `figure.quad` glyph which is used by Holoviews) whether you create a multi-cell quad or aggregating several quads.

MRE:

```python
import numpy as np
import holoviews as hv

hv.extension('bokeh')

n = 8  # Number of bins in each direction
xs = np.logspace(1, 3, n)
ys = np.linspace(1, 10, n)
zs = np.arange((n-1)**2).reshape(n-1, n-1)
hv.QuadMesh((xs, ys, zs)).opts(line_width=0, line_alpha=0)
```

values","user":{"login":"maximlt","id":35924738,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM1OTI0NzM4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/35924738?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt","html_url":"https://github.com/maximlt","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"While working on modernizing the NYC Buildings example (https://github.com/holoviz-topics/examples/pull/386), I noticed that the creation of a HoloViews object (not the rendering) was very slow, the code below took  ~35 seconds to run on my machine.

```python
import time
import spatialpandas as spd
import spatialpandas.io
import hvplot.pandas # noqa

sdf = spd.io.read_parquet('/Users/mliquet/dev/examples/nyc_buildings/data/nyc_buildings.parq')
cats = ['unknown'] + list(sdf['type'].value_counts().iloc[:10].index.values)
sdf['type'] = sdf['type'].replace({None: 'unknown'})
sdf = sdf[sdf['type'].isin(cats)]
sdf['type'] = sdf['type'].astype('category')
sdf = sdf.build_sindex()

start = time.perf_counter()
print("Create object")
p = sdf.hvplot.polygons(rasterize=True, groupby='type', aggregator='any', width=600, height=500)
print(f"Obj creation time: {time.perf_counter() - start}")
```

The new code differs quite a bit from the old one, that was pure HoloViews. Note that the NYC Buildings example uses a DaskSpatialPandas object, I saw the problem was also present for a standard SpatialPandas object.

```python
cats = ['unknown'] + list(sdf['type'].value_counts().iloc[:10].index.values)
polys = hv.Polygons(sdf, vdims='type')
hmap  = hv.HoloMap(OrderedDict([(cat, polys.select(type=cat)) for cat in cats]), 'Type', sort=False)
rasterize(hmap, aggregator='any').opts(width=600, height=500)
```

The main difference is that, internally, hvPlot creates a dynamic HoloViews `groupby` operation. This operation calls the `values` method of the interface, and it turns out that this is very slow for the spatialpandas one, since AFAIU, it has to deal with collecting the values that may be associated with every node of a geometry. This code is in `get_value_array` that loops through every row of the dataset, and every node of the geometry, and creates intermediate numpy arrays that are finally combined based on how the function is called.

This PR implements a couple of optimizations, the main one being in https://github.com/holoviz/holoviews/commit/3fc8935ebbb9772bf5c68296c0b02d9ad51c6f96 that skips the loop entirely if all the values in the targeted column are scalar and if the function should return unique values (`expanded=False`, `expanded=True` is a bit more difficult o deal with as the values must be separated with `np.nan`, and not the scope of this PR). With this change, the timing went down to 0.4s, about 90x faster spatialpandas unique scalar values","user":{"login":"maximlt","id":35924738,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM1OTI0NzM4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/35924738?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt","html_url":"https://github.com/maximlt","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"While working on modernizing the NYC Buildings example (https://github.com/holoviz-topics/examples/pull/386), I noticed that the creation of a HoloViews object (not the rendering) was very slow, the code below took  ~35 seconds to run on my machine.

```python
import time
import spatialpandas as spd
import spatialpandas.io
import hvplot.pandas # noqa

sdf = spd.io.read_parquet('/Users/mliquet/dev/examples/nyc_buildings/data/nyc_buildings.parq')
cats = ['unknown'] + list(sdf['type'].value_counts().iloc[:10].index.values)
sdf['type'] = sdf['type'].replace({None: 'unknown'})
sdf = sdf[sdf['type'].isin(cats)]
sdf['type'] = sdf['type'].astype('category')
sdf = sdf.build_sindex()

start = time.perf_counter()
print("Create object")
p = sdf.hvplot.polygons(rasterize=True, groupby='type', aggregator='any', width=600, height=500)
print(f"Obj creation time: {time.perf_counter() - start}")
```

The new code differs quite a bit from the old one, that was pure HoloViews. Note that the NYC Buildings example uses a DaskSpatialPandas object, I saw the problem was also present for a standard SpatialPandas object.

```python
cats = ['unknown'] + list(sdf['type'].value_counts().iloc[:10].index.values)
polys = hv.Polygons(sdf, vdims='type')
hmap  = hv.HoloMap(OrderedDict([(cat, polys.select(type=cat)) for cat in cats]), 'Type', sort=False)
rasterize(hmap, aggregator='any').opts(width=600, height=500)
```

The main difference is that, internally, hvPlot creates a dynamic HoloViews `groupby` operation. This operation calls the `values` method of the interface, and it turns out that this is very slow for the spatialpandas one, since AFAIU, it has to deal with collecting the values that may be associated with every node of a geometry. This code is in `get_value_array` that loops through every row of the dataset, and every node of the geometry, and creates intermediate numpy arrays that are finally combined based on how the function is called.

This PR implements a couple of optimizations, the main one being in https://github.com/holoviz/holoviews/commit/3fc8935ebbb9772bf5c68296c0b02d9ad51c6f96 that skips the loop entirely if all the values in the targeted column are scalar and if the function should return unique values (`expanded=False`, `expanded=True` is a bit more difficult o deal with as the values must be separated with `np.nan`, and not the scope of this PR). With this change, the timing went down to 0.4s, about 90x {"action":"closed","number":6470,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/holoviz/holoviews/pulls/6470","id":2222164522,"node_id":"PR_kwDOASoy8M6Ec4oq","html_url":"https://github.com/holoviz/holoviews/pull/6470","diff_url":"https://github.com/holoviz/holoviews/pull/6470.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/holoviz/holoviews/pull/6470.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/holoviz/holoviews/issues/6470","number":6470,"state":"closed","locked":false,"title":"perf: faster spatialpandas unique scalar values","user":{"login":"maximlt","id":35924738,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM1OTI0NzM4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/35924738?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt","html_url":"https://github.com/maximlt","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/maximlt/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"While working on modernizing the NYC Buildings example (https://github.com/holoviz-topics/examples/pull/386), I noticed that the creation of a HoloViews object (not the rendering) was very slow, the code below took  ~35 seconds to run on my machine.

```python
import time
import spatialpandas as spd
import spatialpandas.io
import hvplot.pandas # noqa

sdf = spd.io.read_parquet('/Users/mliquet/dev/examples/nyc_buildings/data/nyc_buildings.parq')
cats = ['unknown'] + list(sdf['type'].value_counts().iloc[:10].index.values)
sdf['type'] = sdf['type'].replace({None: 'unknown'})
sdf = sdf[sdf['type'].isin(cats)]
sdf['type'] = sdf['type'].astype('category')
sdf = sdf.build_sindex()

start = time.perf_counter()
print("Create object")
p = sdf.hvplot.polygons(rasterize=True, groupby='type', aggregator='any', width=600, height=500)
print(f"Obj creation time: {time.perf_counter() - start}")
```

The new code differs quite a bit from the old one, that was pure HoloViews. Note that the NYC Buildings example uses a DaskSpatialPandas object, I saw the problem was also present for a standard SpatialPandas object.

```python
cats = ['unknown'] + list(sdf['type'].value_counts().iloc[:10].index.values)
polys = hv.Polygons(sdf, vdims='type')
hmap  = hv.HoloMap(OrderedDict([(cat, polys.select(type=cat)) for cat in cats]), 'Type', sort=False)
rasterize(hmap, aggregator='any').opts(width=600, height=500)
```

The main difference is that, internally, hvPlot creates a dynamic HoloViews `groupby` operation. This operation calls the `values` method of the interface, and it turns out that this is very slow for the spatialpandas one, since AFAIU, it has to deal with collecting the values that may be associated with every node of a geometry. This code is in `get_value_array` that loops through every row of the dataset, and every node of the geometry, and creates intermediate numpy arrays that are finally combined based on how the function is called.

This PR implements a couple of optimizations, the main one being in https://github.com/holoviz/holoviews/commit/3fc8935ebbb9772bf5c68296c0b02d9ad51c6f96 that skips the loop entirely if all the values in the targeted column are scalar and if the function should return unique values (`expanded=False`, `expanded=True` is a bit more difficult o deal with as the values must be separated with `np.nan`, and not the scope of this PR). With this change, the timing went down to 0.4s, about 90x hvplot"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-12-08T20:55:08Z","updated_at":"2024-12-09T18:20:00Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Noticed while modernizing NYC Buildings (https://github.com/holoviz-topics/examples/pull/386), it would be nice to be able to declare whether to sort or not the values of the dimensions used in a `groupby` operation.

```python
import holoviews as hv
import pandas as pd

hv.extension('bokeh')

N = 12
df = pd.DataFrame({
    'cat1': ['A', 'A', 'A', 'A', 'C', 'C', 'C', 'C', 'B', 'B', 'B', 'B'],
    'cat2': ['y', 'y', 'x', 'x', 'y', 'y', 'x', 'x','y', 'y', 'x', 'x'],
    'x'  : range(N),
    'y'  : range(N),
})

hv.Points(df, ['x', 'y'], ['cat1', 'cat2']).groupby(['cat1', 'cat2'], dynamic=True)
```

Order is `ACB`, I'd like to sort to `ABC`.

we sent all hover data to the front end, which could lead to significant overhead on larger plots. With this PR, the data is pushed server-side to the hover tool with a custom data model.  

<details>
<summary>script</summary>

``` python
import itertools

import datashader as ds
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import panel as pn

import holoviews as hv
from holoviews.operation.datashader import datashade, dynspread, rasterize

hv.extension("bokeh")

num = 10000
seed = np.random.default_rng(1)


dists = {
    cat: pd.DataFrame(
        {
            "x": seed.normal(x, s, num),
            "y": seed.normal(y, s, num),
            "s": s,
            "val": val,
            "cat": cat,
        }
    )
    for x, y, s, val, cat in [
        (2, 2, 0.03, 0, "d1"),
        (2, -2, 0.10, 1, "d2"),
        (-2, -2, 0.50, 2, "d3"),
        (-2, 2, 1.00, 3, "d4"),
        (0, 0, 3.00, 4, "d5"),
    ]
}

df = pd.concat(dists, ignore_index=True)
df["y"] += 100  # Offset to distinguish x from y
points = hv.Points(df)


def dynspread_datashade(*args, **kwargs):
    return dynspread(datashade(*args, **kwargs))


def dynspread_rasterize(*args, **kwargs):
    return dynspread(rasterize(*args, **kwargs))


ops = (rasterize, dynspread_rasterize, datashade, dynspread_datashade)
aggs = (ds.min("s"), ds.by("cat"))
sels = (None, ds.min("s"))
# ops = (datashade,)
# aggs = (ds.by("cat"),)
# sels = (None,)
combinations = itertools.product(ops, aggs, sels)
plots = []
for op, agg, sel in combinations:
    title = f"{op.__name__.replace('_','+')}(agg={type(agg).__name__}, sel={type(sel).__name__ if sel else None})"
    plot = op(points, aggregator=agg, selector=sel).opts(
        tools=["hover"], title=title, width=400, height=400
    )
    plots.append(plot)

pn.panel(hv.Layout(plots).cols(4).opts(shared_axes=False)).servable()

```

</details>

selector","user":{"login":"hoxbro","id":19758978,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE5NzU4OTc4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/19758978?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro","html_url":"https://github.com/hoxbro","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"Previously, we sent all hover data to the front end, which could lead to significant overhead on larger plots. With this PR, the data is pushed server-side to the hover tool with a custom data model.  

<details>
<summary>script</summary>

``` python
import itertools

import datashader as ds
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import panel as pn

import holoviews as hv
from holoviews.operation.datashader import datashade, dynspread, rasterize

hv.extension("bokeh")

num = 10000
seed = np.random.default_rng(1)


dists = {
    cat: pd.DataFrame(
        {
            "x": seed.normal(x, s, num),
            "y": seed.normal(y, s, num),
            "s": s,
            "val": val,
            "cat": cat,
        }
    )
    for x, y, s, val, cat in [
        (2, 2, 0.03, 0, "d1"),
        (2, -2, 0.10, 1, "d2"),
        (-2, -2, 0.50, 2, "d3"),
        (-2, 2, 1.00, 3, "d4"),
        (0, 0, 3.00, 4, "d5"),
    ]
}

df = pd.concat(dists, ignore_index=True)
df["y"] += 100  # Offset to distinguish x from y
points = hv.Points(df)


def dynspread_datashade(*args, **kwargs):
    return dynspread(datashade(*args, **kwargs))


def dynspread_rasterize(*args, **kwargs):
    return dynspread(rasterize(*args, **kwargs))


ops = (rasterize, dynspread_rasterize, datashade, dynspread_datashade)
aggs = (ds.min("s"), ds.by("cat"))
sels = (None, ds.min("s"))
# ops = (datashade,)
# aggs = (ds.by("cat"),)
# sels = (None,)
combinations = itertools.product(ops, aggs, sels)
plots = []
for op, agg, sel in combinations:
    title = f"{op.__name__.replace('_','+')}(agg={type(agg).__name__}, sel={type(sel).__name__ if sel else None})"
    plot = op(points, aggregator=agg, selector=sel).opts(
        tools=["hover"], title=title, width=400, height=400
    )
    plots.append(plot)

pn.panel(hv.Layout(plots).cols(4).opts(shared_axes=False)).servable()

```

selector","user":{"login":"hoxbro","id":19758978,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE5NzU4OTc4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/19758978?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro","html_url":"https://github.com/hoxbro","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"Previously, we sent all hover data to the front end, which could lead to significant overhead on larger plots. With this PR, the data is pushed server-side to the hover tool with a custom data model.  

<details>
<summary>script</summary>

``` python
import itertools

import datashader as ds
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import panel as pn

import holoviews as hv
from holoviews.operation.datashader import datashade, dynspread, rasterize

hv.extension("bokeh")

num = 10000
seed = np.random.default_rng(1)


dists = {
    cat: pd.DataFrame(
        {
            "x": seed.normal(x, s, num),
            "y": seed.normal(y, s, num),
            "s": s,
            "val": val,
            "cat": cat,
        }
    )
    for x, y, s, val, cat in [
        (2, 2, 0.03, 0, "d1"),
        (2, -2, 0.10, 1, "d2"),
        (-2, -2, 0.50, 2, "d3"),
        (-2, 2, 1.00, 3, "d4"),
        (0, 0, 3.00, 4, "d5"),
    ]
}

df = pd.concat(dists, ignore_index=True)
df["y"] += 100  # Offset to distinguish x from y
points = hv.Points(df)


def dynspread_datashade(*args, **kwargs):
    return dynspread(datashade(*args, **kwargs))


def dynspread_rasterize(*args, **kwargs):
    return dynspread(rasterize(*args, **kwargs))


ops = (rasterize, dynspread_rasterize, datashade, dynspread_datashade)
aggs = (ds.min("s"), ds.by("cat"))
sels = (None, ds.min("s"))
# ops = (datashade,)
# aggs = (ds.by("cat"),)
# sels = (None,)
combinations = itertools.product(ops, aggs, sels)
plots = []
for op, agg, sel in combinations:
    title = f"{op.__name__.replace('_','+')}(agg={type(agg).__name__}, sel={type(sel).__name__ if sel else None})"
    plot = op(points, aggregator=agg, selector=sel).opts(
        tools=["hover"], title=title, width=400, height=400
    )
    plots.append(plot)

pn.panel(hv.Layout(plots).cols(4).opts(shared_axes=False)).servable()

```

selector","user":{"login":"hoxbro","id":19758978,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE5NzU4OTc4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/19758978?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro","html_url":"https://github.com/hoxbro","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"Previously, we sent all hover data to the front end, which could lead to significant overhead on larger plots. With this PR, the data is pushed server-side to the hover tool with a custom data model.  

<details>
<summary>script</summary>

``` python
import itertools

import datashader as ds
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import panel as pn

import holoviews as hv
from holoviews.operation.datashader import datashade, dynspread, rasterize

hv.extension("bokeh")

num = 10000
seed = np.random.default_rng(1)


dists = {
    cat: pd.DataFrame(
        {
            "x": seed.normal(x, s, num),
            "y": seed.normal(y, s, num),
            "s": s,
            "val": val,
            "cat": cat,
        }
    )
    for x, y, s, val, cat in [
        (2, 2, 0.03, 0, "d1"),
        (2, -2, 0.10, 1, "d2"),
        (-2, -2, 0.50, 2, "d3"),
        (-2, 2, 1.00, 3, "d4"),
        (0, 0, 3.00, 4, "d5"),
    ]
}

df = pd.concat(dists, ignore_index=True)
df["y"] += 100  # Offset to distinguish x from y
points = hv.Points(df)


def dynspread_datashade(*args, **kwargs):
    return dynspread(datashade(*args, **kwargs))


def dynspread_rasterize(*args, **kwargs):
    return dynspread(rasterize(*args, **kwargs))


ops = (rasterize, dynspread_rasterize, datashade, dynspread_datashade)
aggs = (ds.min("s"), ds.by("cat"))
sels = (None, ds.min("s"))
# ops = (datashade,)
# aggs = (ds.by("cat"),)
# sels = (None,)
combinations = itertools.product(ops, aggs, sels)
plots = []
for op, agg, sel in combinations:
    title = f"{op.__name__.replace('_','+')}(agg={type(agg).__name__}, sel={type(sel).__name__ if sel else None})"
    plot = op(points, aggregator=agg, selector=sel).opts(
        tools=["hover"], title=title, width=400, height=400
    )
    plots.append(plot)

pn.panel(hv.Layout(plots).cols(4).opts(shared_axes=False)).servable()

```

we sent all hover data to the front end, which could lead to significant overhead on larger plots. With this PR, the data is pushed server-side to the hover tool with a custom data model.  

<details>
<summary>script</summary>

``` python
import itertools

import datashader as ds
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import panel as pn

import holoviews as hv
from holoviews.operation.datashader import datashade, dynspread, rasterize

hv.extension("bokeh")

num = 10000
seed = np.random.default_rng(1)


dists = {
    cat: pd.DataFrame(
        {
            "x": seed.normal(x, s, num),
            "y": seed.normal(y, s, num),
            "s": s,
            "val": val,
            "cat": cat,
        }
    )
    for x, y, s, val, cat in [
        (2, 2, 0.03, 0, "d1"),
        (2, -2, 0.10, 1, "d2"),
        (-2, -2, 0.50, 2, "d3"),
        (-2, 2, 1.00, 3, "d4"),
        (0, 0, 3.00, 4, "d5"),
    ]
}

df = pd.concat(dists, ignore_index=True)
df["y"] += 100  # Offset to distinguish x from y
points = hv.Points(df)


def dynspread_datashade(*args, **kwargs):
    return dynspread(datashade(*args, **kwargs))


def dynspread_rasterize(*args, **kwargs):
    return dynspread(rasterize(*args, **kwargs))


ops = (rasterize, dynspread_rasterize, datashade, dynspread_datashade)
aggs = (ds.min("s"), ds.by("cat"))
sels = (None, ds.min("s"))
# ops = (datashade,)
# aggs = (ds.by("cat"),)
# sels = (None,)
combinations = itertools.product(ops, aggs, sels)
plots = []
for op, agg, sel in combinations:
    title = f"{op.__name__.replace('_','+')}(agg={type(agg).__name__}, sel={type(sel).__name__ if sel else None})"
    plot = op(points, aggregator=agg, selector=sel).opts(
        tools=["hover"], title=title, width=400, height=400
    )
    plots.append(plot)

pn.panel(hv.Layout(plots).cols(4).opts(shared_axes=False)).servable()

```

Server side HoverTool for rasterized/datashaded plots with selector","user":{"login":"hoxbro","id":19758978,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE5NzU4OTc4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/19758978?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro","html_url":"https://github.com/hoxbro","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"Previously, we sent all hover data to the front end, which could lead to significant overhead on larger plots. With this PR, the data is pushed server-side to the hover tool with a custom data model.  

<details>
<summary>script</summary>

``` python
import itertools

import datashader as ds
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import panel as pn

import holoviews as hv
from holoviews.operation.datashader import datashade, dynspread, rasterize

hv.extension("bokeh")

num = 10000
seed = np.random.default_rng(1)


dists = {
    cat: pd.DataFrame(
        {
            "x": seed.normal(x, s, num),
            "y": seed.normal(y, s, num),
            "s": s,
            "val": val,
            "cat": cat,
        }
    )
    for x, y, s, val, cat in [
        (2, 2, 0.03, 0, "d1"),
        (2, -2, 0.10, 1, "d2"),
        (-2, -2, 0.50, 2, "d3"),
        (-2, 2, 1.00, 3, "d4"),
        (0, 0, 3.00, 4, "d5"),
    ]
}

df = pd.concat(dists, ignore_index=True)
df["y"] += 100  # Offset to distinguish x from y
points = hv.Points(df)


def dynspread_datashade(*args, **kwargs):
    return dynspread(datashade(*args, **kwargs))


def dynspread_rasterize(*args, **kwargs):
    return dynspread(rasterize(*args, **kwargs))


ops = (rasterize, dynspread_rasterize, datashade, dynspread_datashade)
aggs = (ds.min("s"), ds.by("cat"))
sels = (None, ds.min("s"))
# ops = (datashade,)
# aggs = (ds.by("cat"),)
# sels = (None,)
combinations = itertools.product(ops, aggs, sels)
plots = []
for op, agg, sel in combinations:
    title = f"{op.__name__.replace('_','+')}(agg={type(agg).__name__}, sel={type(sel).__name__ if sel else None})"
    plot = op(points, aggregator=agg, selector=sel).opts(
        tools=["hover"], title=title, width=400, height=400
    )
    plots.append(plot)

pn.panel(hv.Layout(plots).cols(4).opts(shared_axes=False)).servable()

```

The name next to a proposal will be assigned to create a sub-issue, and when that is created, I will replace it with the issue number.

# Effort level: Big

1. Remove IPython magic (@hoxbro)
2. `.options` method (duplicative of `.opts`) (@jlstevens)
3. Limit `.to` API to only be available for `Dataset`  (@hoxbro)

# Effort level: Medium

1. Scrubber (@jlstevens)
2. `color_index` and family (@hoxbro)
    a. Check if it warns (Edit: It does)
    b. If it warns, we could likely remove it in the next minor; otherwise, we should make it warn in the next patch version.
3. Remove automatic `rc-file`  (@hoxbro)
    a. Replace it with `hv.extensions(rc_file="~/.holoviewsrc")`

# Effort level: Small

1. Remove unused configs `future_deprecations` and `warn_options_call` 
2. Remove dash support


---

type hints to `__set__`, `__get__`, and `__init__`.

Haven't tested on old versions, so for now this should be seen as a POC. 



<details>
<summary>Code</summary>


``` python
from __future__ import annotations

from typing import reveal_type
from datetime import date

import param


class MyParams(param.Parameterized):
    a = param.Integer()
    b = param.Integer(default=10)
    c = param.Boolean(default=True)
    d = param.Number()
    e = param.Number(default=10.0)

    f = param.Date()
    g = param.Date(default=date(2020, 1, 1))

    h = param.ClassSelector(default=1, class_=int)


params = MyParams()
reveal_type(params.a)
reveal_type(params.b)
reveal_type(params.c)
reveal_type(params.d)
reveal_type(params.e)
reveal_type(params.f)
reveal_type(params.g)
reveal_type(params.h)

for s in "abcdefgh":
    print(f"params.{s} = {getattr(params, s)}")
```


</details>

the latest version of `hvplot` plotting stacked bars from a DataFrame with multiple columns is broken.

See the [second example](https://hvplot.holoviz.org/user_guide/Plotting.html#bars) in the latest docs.

#### minimal example

```python
from hvplot.sample_data import us_crime

crime = us_crime.read()
print(type(crime))
crime.head()

crime.hvplot.bar(x='Year', y=['Violent crime total', 'Property crime total'],
                 stacked=True, rot=90, width=800, legend='top_left')
```

![grafik](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/02b9e152-27d8-4753-9222-82d8936f55c5)

I `y` is a string and not a list, the bar plot works!

versions:
- holoviews = 1.16.1
- datashader = 0.15.0
- bokeh = 3.1.1

As of Bokeh 3.0, [LaTeX is now supported](https://docs.bokeh.org/en/3.1.1/docs/user_guide/styling/mathtext.html), but I don't think it's supported by holoviews yet. Using `r"Upwelling Longwave Flux W$$m^{-2}$$"` doesn't work for the colorbar label:

![test_olr](https://github.com/holoviz/holoviews/assets/66640080/f7ca8079-2af0-41c3-b4aa-de3bb6ecc3a0)

And using that same label but for the title throws a KeyError:
```

Haven't tested on old versions, so for now this should be seen as a POC. 



<details>
<summary>Code</summary>


``` python
from __future__ import annotations

from typing import reveal_type
from datetime import date

import param


class MyParams(param.Parameterized):
    a = param.Integer()
    b = param.Integer(default=10)
    c = param.Boolean(default=True)
    d = param.Number()
    e = param.Number(default=10.0)

    f = param.Date()
    g = param.Date(default=date(2020, 1, 1))

    h = param.ClassSelector(default=1, class_=int)


params = MyParams()
reveal_type(params.a)
reveal_type(params.b)
reveal_type(params.c)
reveal_type(params.d)
reveal_type(params.e)
reveal_type(params.f)
reveal_type(params.g)
reveal_type(params.h)

for s in "abcdefgh":
    print(f"params.{s} = {getattr(params, s)}")
```

from __future__ import annotations

from typing import reveal_type
from datetime import date

import param


class MyParams(param.Parameterized):
    a = param.Integer()
    b = param.Integer(default=10)
    c = param.Boolean(default=True)
    d = param.Number()

Type information shows what the argument is, and type validation verifies that it matches what is expected. This PR currently adds information about the parameter. 

This is useful when running an LSP, as it currently always writes something like `_Undefined | Any | object`; it will now infer it from the `__init__(default=...)`, `__set__`, or `__get__`.

This means an LSP will understand that `a = param.String()` is a string and can autocomplete its method.

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ce5a9670-22ae-45e5-b9b8-f712e7216164)


This does not do any validation. E.g., `param.Integer(default="1")` will not raise any warnings in the code.  Validation of this is handled at runtime.

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/859665de-ce08-4ca6-b0b7-7bfd2bbbea62)


> Given how big of a topic typing Param is, writing down somewhere (e.g. issue, design doc) the direction we are taking would be worthwhile. I will struggle merging a PR without having the big picture in mind.

Understandable. The technology itself currently limits the big picture. I'm also afraid this will be postponed because we don't have a "perfect" solution. we sent all hover data to the front end, which could lead to significant overhead on larger plots. With this PR, the data is pushed server-side to the hover tool with a custom data model.  

<details>
<summary>script</summary>

``` python
import itertools

import datashader as ds
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import panel as pn

import holoviews as hv
from holoviews.operation.datashader import datashade, dynspread, rasterize

hv.extension("bokeh")

num = 10000
seed = np.random.default_rng(1)


dists = {
    cat: pd.DataFrame(
        {
            "x": seed.normal(x, s, num),
            "y": seed.normal(y, s, num),
            "s": s,
            "val": val,
            "cat": cat,
        }
    )
    for x, y, s, val, cat in [
        (2, 2, 0.03, 0, "d1"),
        (2, -2, 0.10, 1, "d2"),
        (-2, -2, 0.50, 2, "d3"),
        (-2, 2, 1.00, 3, "d4"),
        (0, 0, 3.00, 4, "d5"),
    ]
}

df = pd.concat(dists, ignore_index=True)
df["y"] += 100  # Offset to distinguish x from y
points = hv.Points(df)


def dynspread_datashade(*args, **kwargs):
    return dynspread(datashade(*args, **kwargs))


def dynspread_rasterize(*args, **kwargs):
    return dynspread(rasterize(*args, **kwargs))


ops = (rasterize, dynspread_rasterize, datashade, dynspread_datashade)
aggs = (ds.min("s"), ds.by("cat"))
sels = (None, ds.min("s"))
# ops = (datashade,)
# aggs = (ds.by("cat"),)
# sels = (None,)
combinations = itertools.product(ops, aggs, sels)
plots = []
for op, agg, sel in combinations:
    title = f"{op.__name__.replace('_','+')}(agg={type(agg).__name__}, sel={type(sel).__name__ if sel else None})"
    plot = op(points, aggregator=agg, selector=sel).opts(
        tools=["hover"], title=title, width=400, height=400
    )
    plots.append(plot)

pn.panel(hv.Layout(plots).cols(4).opts(shared_axes=False)).servable()

```

</details>

Server side HoverTool for rasterized/datashaded plots with selector","user":{"login":"hoxbro","id":19758978,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE5NzU4OTc4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/19758978?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro","html_url":"https://github.com/hoxbro","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hoxbro/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"Previously, we sent all hover data to the front end, which could lead to significant overhead on larger plots. With this PR, the data is pushed server-side to the hover tool with a custom data model.  

<details>
<summary>script</summary>

``` python
import itertools

import datashader as ds
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import panel as pn

import holoviews as hv
from holoviews.operation.datashader import datashade, dynspread, rasterize

hv.extension("bokeh")

num = 10000
seed = np.random.default_rng(1)


dists = {
    cat: pd.DataFrame(
        {
            "x": seed.normal(x, s, num),
            "y": seed.normal(y, s, num),
            "s": s,
            "val": val,
            "cat": cat,
        }
    )
    for x, y, s, val, cat in [
        (2, 2, 0.03, 0, "d1"),
        (2, -2, 0.10, 1, "d2"),
        (-2, -2, 0.50, 2, "d3"),
        (-2, 2, 1.00, 3, "d4"),
        (0, 0, 3.00, 4, "d5"),
    ]
}

df = pd.concat(dists, ignore_index=True)
df["y"] += 100  # Offset to distinguish x from y
points = hv.Points(df)


def dynspread_datashade(*args, **kwargs):
    return dynspread(datashade(*args, **kwargs))


def dynspread_rasterize(*args, **kwargs):
    return dynspread(rasterize(*args, **kwargs))


ops = (rasterize, dynspread_rasterize, datashade, dynspread_datashade)
aggs = (ds.min("s"), ds.by("cat"))
sels = (None, ds.min("s"))
# ops = (datashade,)
# aggs = (ds.by("cat"),)
# sels = (None,)
combinations = itertools.product(ops, aggs, sels)
plots = []
for op, agg, sel in combinations:
    title = f"{op.__name__.replace('_','+')}(agg={type(agg).__name__}, sel={type(sel).__name__ if sel else None})"
    plot = op(points, aggregator=agg, selector=sel).opts(
        tools=["hover"], title=title, width=400, height=400
    )
    plots.append(plot)

pn.panel(hv.Layout(plots).cols(4).opts(shared_axes=False)).servable()

```

</details>

type hints to `__set__`, `__get__`, and `__init__`.

Haven't tested on old versions, so for now this should be seen as a POC. 



<details>
<summary>Code</summary>


``` python
from __future__ import annotations

from typing import reveal_type
from datetime import date

import param


class MyParams(param.Parameterized):
    a = param.Integer()
    b = param.Integer(default=10)
    c = param.Boolean(default=True)
    d = param.Number()
    e = param.Number(default=10.0)

    f = param.Date()
    g = param.Date(default=date(2020, 1, 1))

    h = param.ClassSelector(default=1, class_=int)


params = MyParams()
reveal_type(params.a)
reveal_type(params.b)
reveal_type(params.c)
reveal_type(params.d)
reveal_type(params.e)
reveal_type(params.f)
reveal_type(params.g)
reveal_type(params.h)

for s in "abcdefgh":
    print(f"params.{s} = {getattr(params, s)}")
```


</details>

We can get type validation. I'm using [basedpyright](https://docs.basedpyright.com/latest/) as my LSP here. Setting the type-checking mode to "basic" (lowest type setting, see [here](https://docs.basedpyright.com/latest/configuration/config-files/)) with the diff in https://github.com/holoviz/param/pull/985#issuecomment-2546286190, give the following result (note I have only activated this mode in this comment):

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5bbd0abb-82ca-4db3-90c7-4e6b3ea5673e)


>>    We likely could add some validation, this will, as far as I can see, come at the cost of type information (which could be ok).
>
> Why?

As you can see, every `reveal_type` outputs `int`, even though `d` is actually a string. This is what I meant by trade-off: we get some type-validation at the cost of wrong type-information. This will error out at runtime, so this is OK. Ideally the type-validation and runtime-validation would match up, but there could be edge cases.  Also, currently, we will lose some of the type information when doing it with a bit more complex parameter like `param.List`.

> > This is also not supported, and I am wondering if it can be supported.
> 
> I'm not sure I follow, what is not supported?

See `c` in the screenshot above, e.g.,  `c: int`. Sorry for not being more specific.


> Which technology? How Param is implemented? What Python typing offers? Or both?

Mostly, Python typing is pretty rough. Some changes could likely also be done in Param. 

> Don't be afraid :) You've already mentioned a few interesting things in this PR (type information, LSP, type validation, dataclass_transform),

I haven't found a use for `dataclass_transform` yet, but it could come into play later. 

>  it'd be valuable to collect them in a single document that can be augmented over time with our findings. 

It would be ideal, but currently, I'm just trying things out and seeing what sticks. I'm not even sure this works with other people's setups.
